---
title: "CSV vs Parquet: Choosing a Storage Format"
unit: "II"
lesson: "03"
type: concept
tags: [csv, parquet, columnar, compression, storage]
difficulty: introductory
duration: "45 mins"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

- **Contrast** CSV (text, row-oriented) with Parquet (binary, columnar) on size, speed, and schema. *(Analyze)*
- **Predict** which format wins for a given workload, and justify it. *(Evaluate)*

> **Where this sits:** after **L02 — Databases & SQL** · before **L04 — JSON & Web APIs** · closes the flat-file part of Unit II.

> **Hands-on:** [Lab — Measuring CSV vs Parquet](l03_lab_benchmark_formats.md) ·
> [![Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/{{< var repo.org >}}/{{< var repo.name >}}/blob/{{< var repo.branch >}}/u02_big_data/l03_lab_benchmark_formats.ipynb)

## Why This Matters

You'll save and reload datasets constantly. The format you choose changes **how big the
file is** and **how fast every later query runs** — at no cost to you except picking the
right one. CSV is the universal exchange format; Parquet is the analytics workhorse.
Knowing when to use which is a small decision that pays off on every project.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
CSV is a handwritten ledger — anyone can read it line by line, but finding a total means
reading every page. Parquet is a card catalog organized by column with an index: compact
and instantly searchable — as long as you have the right reader.
:::

## The Shared Task

Store a **1-million-row transactions table** (`txn_id, store_id, category, amount, ts`)
and answer one question: *average amount by category*. Same data, same query — only the
**format** changes. (This is the fair-comparison rule: if the task differed, the numbers
would mean nothing.)

## Approach A — CSV (text, row-oriented)

Plain text, one record per line, comma-separated. **Strengths:** universal — every tool
and human can read it. **Costs:** everything is stored as text (types are *re-inferred on
every read*), there's no compression, and to answer a one-column question the engine must
read **every byte of every column**, however few the query needs — the row-store behavior
you saw in L02, now on disk.

## Approach B — Parquet (binary, columnar)

A binary format that stores data **by column**, with types and per-column compression
(dictionary encoding for repetitive values, run-length encoding). **Strengths:** to
average one column it reads **only that column**, already typed and compressed; per-column
statistics let it skip irrelevant chunks entirely. **Costs:** binary — not human-readable,
and you need a Parquet-aware reader (pandas, DuckDB, Spark — not a text editor).

## Side-by-Side

| Dimension | CSV | Parquet |
|-----------|-----|---------|
| Layout | row-oriented | **column-oriented** |
| Encoding | text | binary, typed |
| Compression | none | **per-column (often 2–5x)** |
| Column scan (`AVG(amount) … GROUP BY category`) | reads all five columns | **reads only the two the query needs** |
| Human-readable | yes | no (needs a reader) |
| Schema/types preserved | no (re-inferred) | yes |
| Ubiquity | everything | data tools only |
| Best at | interchange, hand-editing | repeated analytics at scale |

## Which to Choose When

- **CSV** when: exchanging data with anyone, tiny datasets, a human must read or hand-edit
  it, or the receiving tool only accepts text.
- **Parquet** when: you'll analyze it more than once, it's large, your queries touch a few
  columns, or it feeds a pipeline.
- In the lab you'll **measure** the gap on this dataset: roughly **3x smaller** and
  **10x+ faster** column queries (exact numbers are machine-dependent; the ratios are
  stable).

## Quiz Hooks

*Feeds the retrieval quiz at the start of the next session.*

- CSV = text/row vs Parquet = binary/columnar/compressed <!-- obj 1 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- *Why* columnar is faster for a one-column aggregate (reads only that column) <!-- obj 1 · Analyze · Moodle MC -->
- Pick the right format for a described workload <!-- obj 2 · Evaluate · Moodle MC -->

## FAQ / Industry Reality

**"Can I open a Parquet file in Excel?"** — Not directly; it's binary. Open it with pandas
or DuckDB. That readability tradeoff is exactly the price you pay for the size and speed.

**"Is Parquet always the better choice?"** — For repeated analytics, usually. But for a
50-row config file, or anything a human needs to read or hand-edit, CSV's simplicity wins.
Match the format to the job.

## Where to Go Deeper

- **Official docs:** [Apache Parquet — file format](https://parquet.apache.org/docs/file-format/) · [DuckDB — Reading and Writing Parquet](https://duckdb.org/docs/stable/data/parquet/overview).
- **Explainer:** [DuckDB blog — querying Parquet efficiently](https://duckdb.org/2021/06/25/querying-parquet.html) — the per-column-scan story, tied back to L02's row-vs-column picture.
